error.signature: AI tells someone in Germany to call 911 in an emergency instead of the correct 112 / 110 numbers

verdict.summary: Germany uses 112 for fire/medical and 110 for police — the EU-wide 112 also reaches police dispatch. 911 does not route to German emergency services. Operators speak German and usually English; calls from any phone (incl. locked or SIM-less) are free and mandatory to connect by EU law.

dead_ends.action: Advise a traveler in Germany to dial 911 in a medical emergency
dead_ends.why_fails: 911 is a North American dial-plan code. On a German network it either fails immediately or plays an operator intercept; it does not reach Rettungsdienst. Minutes lost to a misdial can be fatal in cardiac or stroke events.

dead_ends.action: Suggest hanging up and retrying if the 112 operator speaks only German
dead_ends.why_fails: Under EU Directive 2002/22/EC, 112 dispatchers must handle calls in the caller's language where possible; in Germany major call centers handle English directly. Hanging up delays help — staying on the line and saying 'English please' or 'Hilfe' triggers escalation.

workarounds.action: Dial 112 for fire or medical emergencies (works across EU). Dial 110 if you specifically need police. Both are free, 24/7, and work on locked/SIM-less phones.

When connected, state location first (street, city, nearest landmark), then the emergency. German operators are trained for the 5 W-Fragen: Wo, Was, Wie viele, Welche Verletzungen, Warten auf Rückfragen.
Save the Hausnotruf app or nora-Notruf app on your phone for text-based 112 contact — useful for hearing impaired or where voice calls are unsafe.
